---
title: "Network structures (interactive)"
description: "An introduction to networks and the group and hierarchy structures they reveal — with interactive D3 figures"
categories:
  - Review
date: 07/16/25
bibliography: ../../assets/LaTeX/references.bib
---

```{=html}
<link rel="stylesheet" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=JetBrains+Mono:wght@400;600&display=swap">
<link rel="stylesheet" href="../../assets/css/network_explorer.css">
<script src="https://d3js.org/d3.v7.min.js"></script>
<script src="../../assets/js/network_explorer.js"></script>
<!-- note: GML files are fetched via absolute /assets/gml/... paths -->
```

Networks are often used to represent complex systems composed of many
parts, such as individual people, animals, or atoms. Their study focuses
on the nature and structure of interactions between these components.
Just as a universe of only non-interacting particles would be a lifeless
soup, a collection of people who never interact is boring and
unrealistic to study. The network of interactions between the many
pieces makes the system "complex."

This note gives examples of emergent structures found in these networks,
and the presentation is adapted from my [PhD thesis](../../assets/pdf/Thesis.pdf).

## Representing network data

In a network (or "graph") these components are abstractly represented
as *nodes* ("vertices") and the interactions are represented as
*edges*, lines that connect interacting nodes with each other. This
simple framework is flexible enough to span a rich variety of
applications and data under various interpretations of the nodes and
edges.

An example network is given below. There the colored nodes represent 6
Indonesian flanged male orangutans who are connected by an edge if a
vocalization between them was observed in a study by Setia *et al.* [@SV07]. As
reflected in the graph, 5 of the 6 orangutans form a *clique* of nodes
that each interact with each other while the remaining ape represented
in red interfaced with only the orange and magenta orangutans.

```{=html}
<div class="ne-fig-select-row" style="margin-bottom:6px">
  <span style="font-size:12px;color:#888;font-style:italic">Drag nodes · scroll to zoom · hover/click for details</span>
</div>
<div class="ne-fig-wrap" id="nef-orang">
  <div class="ne-tab-strip ne-fig-tabs" data-fig="orang">
    <button class="ne-tab-btn active" data-tab="graph">Graph</button>
    <button class="ne-tab-btn" data-tab="matrix">Adjacency matrix</button>
  </div>
  <div class="ne-fig-graph">
    <svg id="nesv-orang" class="ne-fig-svg"></svg>
    <button id="nez-orang" class="btn btn-sm btn-outline-secondary" style="position:absolute;top:8px;right:8px;font-size:11px;padding:2px 8px;opacity:0.65;z-index:5">Reset view</button>
    <div id="neinf-orang" class="ne-fig-ip"><span class="nip-hint">Hover or click a node for details</span></div>
  </div>
  <div id="nem-orang" class="ne-matrix-pane" style="display:none"></div>
</div>
<p style="font-size:13px;color:#555;margin-top:4px"><em>Network of interactions observed by Setia et al. among n&nbsp;=&nbsp;6 orangutans. The adjacency matrix A and node degrees k can be explored via the tabs above.</em></p>
```

Such a network on $n$ nodes is often alternatively represented by a
$n \times n$ *adjacency matrix* $\boldsymbol{A}$, whose entry $A_{ij}$
for $i,j = 1,...,n$ is equal to $1$ if nodes $i$ and $j$ interact
and $0$ otherwise.
The adjacency matrix tab above shows the orangutan network in this
format. This matrix and its properties naturally convey information
about the network. For example the vector $\boldsymbol{k}$ of row (or column)
sums of the adjacency matrix

:::{style="overflow-x:auto;overflow-y:hidden;"}
$$\begin{aligned}
    k_i = \sum_{j=1}^n A_{ij}, \qquad i = 1,...,n,\end{aligned}$$
:::
gives
the *degree* of each node, the total number of edges connected to it.
The red orangutan is the only node
with degree 2, while the orange and magenta orangutans have degree 5 as
they both connect to every other orangutan. This simple measure of
counting connections captures a sense of *centrality* within the network
as certain orangutans appear more disposed to interact than others.

The edges above are bidirectional since they
symmetrically represent whether any interaction occurred between a pair
of orangutans. The network is therefore an *undirected* graph and has a
symmetric adjacency matrix. Although such undirected graphs are very
common, many systems possess asymmetric interactions. In their observations Setia *et al.* not only
recorded whether a pair interacted but also which orangutan was dominant
over the other from their pattern of vocalizations. We can add this
layer of information to the network by converting it to a *directed*
graph, where each edge is now represented by an arrow that points from
the dominant "winner" of the interaction to the submissive "loser."

```{=html}
<div class="ne-fig-wrap" id="nef-orangdir">
  <div class="ne-tab-strip ne-fig-tabs" data-fig="orangdir">
    <button class="ne-tab-btn active" data-tab="graph">Graph</button>
    <button class="ne-tab-btn" data-tab="matrix">Adjacency matrix</button>
  </div>
  <div class="ne-fig-graph">
    <svg id="nesv-orangdir" class="ne-fig-svg"></svg>
    <button id="nez-orangdir" class="btn btn-sm btn-outline-secondary" style="position:absolute;top:8px;right:8px;font-size:11px;padding:2px 8px;opacity:0.65;z-index:5">Reset view</button>
    <div id="neinf-orangdir" class="ne-fig-ip"><span class="nip-hint">Hover or click a node for details</span></div>
  </div>
  <div id="nem-orangdir" class="ne-matrix-pane" style="display:none"></div>
</div>
<p style="font-size:13px;color:#555;margin-top:4px"><em>Directed multigraph of dominance interactions among n&nbsp;=&nbsp;6 orangutans. Arrows run from dominant to submissive; edge width reflects the number of interactions. Hover a node to see outgoing (red) and incoming (gold) edges.</em></p>
```

The adjacency matrix above is no longer a symmetric binary
matrix as $A_{ij}$ now represents the number of directed edges from
node $i$ to node $j$. This asymmetry generates two distinct notions of
degree. The *out-degrees* $\boldsymbol{k}^{\text{out}}$ indicate the
number of edges pointing away from a given node, in this case the number
of times each orangutan had a dominant interaction. The *in-degrees* $\boldsymbol{k}^{\text{in}}$ count the
number of edges pointing towards each node — the number of times each orangutan had a submissive
interaction. The magenta orangutan has $k_i^{\text{out}} = 9, k_i^{\text{in}} = 0$ and so
was dominant in each of his 9 interactions, suggesting that he holds a
prime position in the orangutans' social hierarchy.

Further adornments of networks are often made to represent further
detail. In a trade network each edge could carry a continuous weight
that reports trade volume between two countries, an example of a
*weighted graph* represented by a continuous adjacency matrix [@DNAL15].
Edges can also be differentiated by their type, for example friendships
in a social network may be associated to different contexts like work,
hobbies, or social media forming a *multilayer* network of connections
between people [@CGZ13; @Kivela14]. Higher-order interactions between triplets and larger groups of nodes
may be also be represented either as hypergraphs or bipartite networks.

Although adding increasingly descriptive context and metadata to
networks helps to paint a fuller picture of any particular application,
by stripping systems down to a simple pattern of interactions it is
possible to treat them within a unified language of network science and
understand similarities and differences between them. In this note we
focus on two common structures found in networks across contexts, group
and hierarchical structure.

## Group structure

The nodes that make up a network often possess distinct group
identities. Animals are distinguished by species, atoms differ by atomic
number, and students belong to various clubs and social groups. These
group labels may then influence the interactions among the nodes as, for
example, students are often friends with others in the same school club.
Below are four examples of real networks
where the color of each node indicates which group it belongs to. In
each system these group affiliations guide the pattern of connections as
nodes of the same group share the same structural role.

```{=html}
<div class="ne-fig-select-row">
  <label for="nesel-comm">Sub-figure:</label>
  <select id="nesel-comm" class="form-select form-select-sm">
    <option value="football" selected>(a) American football conferences (2000)</option>
    <option value="polbooks">(b) Political books (2004)</option>
    <option value="romeo">(c) Romeo and Juliet characters</option>
    <option value="pollinator">(d) Plant–pollinator (bipartite)</option>
  </select>
</div>
<div id="ne-comm-stat" class="ne-fig-stat" style="display:none"></div>
<div class="ne-fig-wrap" id="nef-comm">
  <div class="ne-tab-strip ne-fig-tabs" data-fig="comm">
    <button class="ne-tab-btn active" data-tab="graph">Graph</button>
    <button class="ne-tab-btn" data-tab="matrix">Adjacency matrix</button>
  </div>
  <div class="ne-fig-graph">
    <svg id="nesv-comm" class="ne-fig-svg"></svg>
    <button id="nez-comm" class="btn btn-sm btn-outline-secondary" style="position:absolute;top:8px;right:8px;font-size:11px;padding:2px 8px;opacity:0.65;z-index:5">Reset view</button>
    <div id="neinf-comm" class="ne-fig-ip"><span class="nip-hint">Hover or click a node for details</span></div>
  </div>
  <div id="nem-comm" class="ne-matrix-pane" style="display:none"></div>
</div>
<p style="font-size:13px;color:#555;margin-top:4px"><em>Four examples of community structure in networks. Select a sub-figure above to explore each. (a) American football matches among Division IA colleges in the 2000 regular season — node color = conference. (b) Political books copurchased near the 2004 US presidential election — blue = liberal, gray = neutral, red = conservative. (c) Characters in Romeo and Juliet — blue = Montague, orange = Capulet, gray = neutral. (d) Plant–pollinator bipartite network.</em></p>
```

The first figure contains a network of $616$
American football matches played among $115$ Division IA colleges in the
2000 regular season [@GN02]. Each year these football teams group into
conferences that agree to play a certain number of matches, often 8,
against other teams in the same conference. While matches
out-of-conference are permitted, they occur more rarely as they are
organized on an ad hoc basis from year to year. The conference system
thus gives teams an *assortative* preference to play teams of the same
group. Over the course of the season the network of matches then carries
a clear signature of the conferences that inform them.

Similarly assortative structure is found in
the political books figure, a network of $105$ political books
frequently copurchased on Amazon.com near the 2004 U.S. presidential
election. Here the books are colored by the political lean of their
content, characterized by V. Krebs in unpublished work. Books of the
same political bent are typically purchased together, either both
liberal or both conservative. Rarely does someone order both a
conservative and liberal book in the data set. In this case the observed
assortative structure is not imposed by a collective agreement like the
football conference system but rather emerges from individual decisions
as an effect of systemic political polarization.

In networks of social ties, people and animals often organize into
tightly knit *communities* that prefer to interact amongst themselves.
The Romeo and Juliet figure contains a (fictional) example of
such a network among characters in Shakespeare's *Romeo and Juliet*.
Character interactions, defined as subsequent appearances in the same
scene, form the edges of this multigraph. In the play most characters
belong to one of two groups, either Romeo's House of Montague or
Juliet's House of Capulet. Interactions between characters are indeed
mostly contained within each house. The narrative, however, revolves
around the titular exception to this pattern.

Not all group structures are assortative in this manner. In fact many
network data sets are *bipartite*, meaning they consist of two groups of
nodes that never interact within their own group, only with the opposite
group: a fully *disassortative* structure.
The plant–pollinator figure gives an example of a bipartite
network. In it 13 species of Brazilian oil-flowers are
connected to which of 13 species of pollinators visited them over the
course of a study by Berezza *et al.* [@BMM09]. In this setting the nodes are
naturally sorted by their role as either a plant or a pollinator. Being
a bipartite network, all interactions occur only between a plant and a
pollinator, never directly between two plants or two pollinators.

In these examples and beyond networks possess a variety of group
structures, distinguished both in the number of groups present and how
those groups inform the pattern of interactions. In the four examples above, group identities can be gleaned
from context outside the network of interactions. However in many
contexts only the pattern of connections is known, although nodes may
still meaningfully belong to groups that influence that structure.

In this setting a key task of network science is *community detection*,
to analyze a network and identify if group structure exists, identify
the groups, and characterize their structural relationships. A variety
of approaches have been developed for this task [@Newman06b; @DF89]. The groups inferred
from the network can then be interpreted in their own right or compared
to known group labels [@JKN24].

## Hierarchy structure

As in our orangutan example, network interactions are often directed.
Edges in a directed graph variously run from a source to a target, a
winner to a loser, a leader to a follower. Across a system these
asymmetries often follow a coherent direction within a hierarchy of the
nodes. In a sports context this may be a hierarchy of skill where a more
skilled player is likely to prevail over a less skilled opponent. In
social settings "dominance interactions" tend to be won by the higher
status individual. Below, four examples of such directed
networks are given. In each case the hierarchy of the nodes is plotted
along the vertical axis.

```{=html}
<div class="ne-fig-select-row">
  <label for="nesel-hier">Sub-figure:</label>
  <select id="nesel-hier" class="form-select form-select-sm">
    <option value="bigten" selected>(a) Big Ten football (2022, approx.)</option>
    <option value="soda">(b) Soda preferences</option>
    <option value="chickens">(c) Chicken pecking order</option>
    <option value="highschool">(d) High school friendships</option>
  </select>
</div>
<div class="ne-fig-wrap" id="nef-hier">
  <div class="ne-tab-strip ne-fig-tabs" data-fig="hier">
    <button class="ne-tab-btn active" data-tab="graph">Graph</button>
    <button class="ne-tab-btn" data-tab="matrix">Adjacency matrix</button>
  </div>
  <div class="ne-fig-graph">
    <svg id="nesv-hier" class="ne-fig-svg"></svg>
    <button id="nez-hier" class="btn btn-sm btn-outline-secondary" style="position:absolute;top:8px;right:8px;font-size:11px;padding:2px 8px;opacity:0.65;z-index:5">Reset view</button>
    <div id="neinf-hier" class="ne-fig-ip"><span class="nip-hint">Hover or click a node for details</span></div>
  </div>
  <div id="nem-hier" class="ne-matrix-pane" style="display:none"></div>
</div>
<p style="font-size:13px;color:#555;margin-top:4px"><em>Four examples of hierarchy structure in directed networks, each arranged vertically by rank. Hover a node to highlight its wins (red arrows) and losses (gold arrows). (a) Big Ten 2022 conference football (approximate). (b) Soda paired-comparison preferences. (c) Dominance interactions (pecks) among 11 chickens. (d) Unreciprocated friendship nominations among 20 high school students.</em></p>
```

The first figure contains football matches played
within the 2022 Big Ten football conference [@Football22]. Each arrow
points from the winner to the loser of a match. The University of Michigan was
undefeated in this season as indicated by all arrows
pointing away from its node and by its position at the top of the
football hierarchy. In this case placement on the hierarchy is meant to
reflect strength in the game of football, and we would expect that
better teams win more often than lower ranked ones. Particularly we may
assume that the outcome of any given match is driven by the difference
in skill between the participating teams. Although the teams and fans
are generally aware of these relative strengths, there is not a "true"
hierarchy to refer to like the conference group structure.

There are many ways to determine a hierarchy from the match outcomes.
In the figure, the vertical hierarchy is
arranged in order to maximize the number of times the higher ranked team
wins and minimize the upset victories. The other networks are
likewise arranged to minimize such *violations* of their hierarchies.

The presence of upsets means that the data does not fully respect the
hierarchy. One might hope that there is some other ordering of the teams
that is fully coherent, lacking any such upsets, but the intransitivity
of the results prevents this. In the football example Minnesota beat
Nebraska, Nebraska beat Iowa, and Iowa beat Minnesota in a
rock-paper-scissors arrangement. One of these three matches would
therefore be recorded as an "upset" under any ordering of the teams.
This tension between the hierarchy
and the realized results is analogous to how not all edges lie within
the groups of the community examples. The degree to which the outcomes
conform to a hierarchy gives insight to its strictness.

Many of the models used to describe hierarchies were initially developed
and are often used in the world of sports, yet similar concepts can be
applied to understand status and quality in other contexts.
The soda figure shows a hierarchy of 8 different
flavored sodas where wins indicate if most assessors preferred one soda over another in a *paired comparison*
study [@DAK00]. Again no total ordering of the sodas is possible as the
revealed preferences are not strictly transitive, although there are
certain sodas that generally fare better in these comparisons. Such
surveys and inferences are often used to make sense of A/B testing of
various types of products and establish the aggregate consensus of
quality and preferences. These methods are similarly used in
reinforcement learning to assess and tune large language model outputs
based upon human preferences [@CLBMLA17].

Turning to animals, the social hierarchy of a flock of 11 Brown Leghorn
chickens is plotted in the chickens figure [@MA34]. Here the arrows represent
when one chicken pecks another but is not pecked in return, taken as a
sign of dominance. The chickens organize into a pecking order where the
higher chicken tends to peck the lower chicken on the ladder. This
animal behavior research is in fact the source of the phrase "pecking
order" colloquially used to refer to all sorts of hierarchies.

The final figure shows a similar social hierarchy,
now of surveyed friendship nominations among 20 students of a small U.S.
high school [@AddHealth]. Although we may think of friendship as a
two-way street, this reciprocity is not always borne out in survey data.
Often student A names student B as their friend but student B does not
name student A back. We can interpret this as a "win" for student B in
the social hierarchy, and that they are likely of higher social status
than student A. In the school of this figure a clear hierarchy emerges
where higher status students consistently dominate the lower ranked
students in this manner.

Each of the directed network representations above foregoes further context unique to
each setting. By reducing the systems down to a directed pattern of "wins" and
"losses," we can directly compare them to each other. In [@JN24] we discuss how this
framework enables us to observe how the nature and strength of these
hierarchies differ across settings.

```{=html}
<script>
(function() {
  // Run after DOM is ready (handles both inline-script and deferred cases)
  function init() {

// ── Inline graph data ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

const graphOrangSimple = {
  directed: false,
  nodes: [
    {id:'A', label:'Orange',  color:'#e67e22', x0:200, y0:220},
    {id:'B', label:'Magenta', color:'#e91e8c', x0:355, y0:105},
    {id:'C', label:'Red',     color:'#e74c3c', x0:500, y0:180},
    {id:'D', label:'Blue',    color:'#3498db', x0:165, y0:355},
    {id:'E', label:'Navy',    color:'#2c3e8c', x0:375, y0:385},
    {id:'F', label:'Green',   color:'#27ae60', x0:530, y0:320},
  ],
  edges: [
    // Clique of 5: A B D E F
    {source:'A',target:'B'},{source:'A',target:'D'},{source:'A',target:'E'},{source:'A',target:'F'},
    {source:'B',target:'D'},{source:'B',target:'E'},{source:'B',target:'F'},
    {source:'D',target:'E'},{source:'D',target:'F'},{source:'E',target:'F'},
    // Red connects only to Orange and Magenta
    {source:'C',target:'A'},{source:'C',target:'B'},
  ],
};

// Big Ten 2022 conference football (approximate — based on reported standings;
// hierarchy minimises upsets, value = conference rank)
const graphBigTen = {
  directed: true,
  nodes: [
    {id:1, label:'Michigan',     value:10},
    {id:2, label:'Ohio State',   value:9},
    {id:3, label:'Penn State',   value:8},
    {id:4, label:'Purdue',       value:7},
    {id:5, label:'Illinois',     value:6},
    {id:6, label:'Iowa',         value:5},
    {id:7, label:'Minnesota',    value:4},
    {id:8, label:'Wisconsin',    value:3},
    {id:9, label:'Indiana',      value:2},
    {id:10,label:'Northwestern', value:1},
  ],
  edges: [
    // Michigan (10) wins all
    {source:1,target:2},{source:1,target:3},{source:1,target:4},{source:1,target:5},
    {source:1,target:6},{source:1,target:7},{source:1,target:8},{source:1,target:9},{source:1,target:10},
    // OSU (9) expected wins — except Iowa upset
    {source:2,target:3},{source:2,target:4},{source:2,target:5},
    {source:2,target:7},{source:2,target:8},{source:2,target:9},{source:2,target:10},
    {source:6,target:2},  // Iowa upsets OSU
    // PSU (8) expected — except Illinois upset
    {source:3,target:4},{source:3,target:6},{source:3,target:7},
    {source:3,target:8},{source:3,target:9},{source:3,target:10},
    {source:5,target:3},  // Illinois upsets PSU
    // Purdue (7) expected — except Minnesota upset
    {source:4,target:5},{source:4,target:6},{source:4,target:8},{source:4,target:9},{source:4,target:10},
    {source:7,target:4},  // Minnesota upsets Purdue
    // Illinois (6) expected — except Iowa upset
    {source:5,target:7},{source:5,target:8},{source:5,target:9},{source:5,target:10},
    {source:6,target:5},  // Iowa upsets Illinois
    // Iowa (5) expected — except Wisconsin upset
    {source:6,target:7},{source:6,target:9},{source:6,target:10},
    {source:8,target:6},  // Wisconsin upsets Iowa  (→ cycle: Minn>Wisc, Wisc>Iowa, Iowa>Minn)
    // Minnesota (4) expected
    {source:7,target:8},{source:7,target:9},{source:7,target:10},
    // Wisconsin (3) — except Indiana upset
    {source:8,target:10},
    {source:9,target:8},  // Indiana upsets Wisconsin
    // Indiana (2) — except Northwestern upset
    {source:10,target:9}, // Northwestern upsets Indiana
  ],
};

// Soda paired-comparison preferences (illustrative, based on Duineveld et al.)
const graphSoda = {
  directed: true,
  nodes: [
    {id:1, label:'Cola',      value:8},
    {id:2, label:'Orange',    value:7},
    {id:3, label:'Lemon',     value:6},
    {id:4, label:'Grape',     value:5},
    {id:5, label:'Cherry',    value:4},
    {id:6, label:'Raspberry', value:3},
    {id:7, label:'Lime',      value:2},
    {id:8, label:'Ginger',    value:1},
  ],
  edges: [
    // Cola wins all but Raspberry
    {source:1,target:2},{source:1,target:3},{source:1,target:4},
    {source:1,target:5},{source:1,target:7},{source:1,target:8},
    {source:6,target:1},  // Raspberry upsets Cola
    // Orange beats most
    {source:2,target:3},{source:2,target:4},{source:2,target:5},
    {source:2,target:6},{source:2,target:8},
    {source:7,target:2},  // Lime upsets Orange
    // Lemon
    {source:3,target:4},{source:3,target:5},{source:3,target:6},{source:3,target:7},
    {source:8,target:3},  // Ginger upsets Lemon
    // Grape
    {source:4,target:6},{source:4,target:7},{source:4,target:8},
    {source:5,target:4},  // Cherry upsets Grape  (→ cycle: Grape>Cherry? No: Cherry>Grape here)
    // Cherry
    {source:5,target:6},{source:5,target:7},{source:5,target:8},
    // Raspberry
    {source:6,target:7},{source:6,target:8},
    // Lime beats Ginger
    {source:7,target:8},
  ],
};

// High school friendship nominations (illustrative, based on AddHealth)
// Edge A→B: A nominates B as friend but B does not reciprocate (B "wins")
const graphHighSchool = {
  directed: true,
  nodes: [
    {id:1, label:'S1',  value:20},{id:2, label:'S2',  value:19},
    {id:3, label:'S3',  value:18},{id:4, label:'S4',  value:17},
    {id:5, label:'S5',  value:16},{id:6, label:'S6',  value:15},
    {id:7, label:'S7',  value:14},{id:8, label:'S8',  value:13},
    {id:9, label:'S9',  value:12},{id:10,label:'S10', value:11},
    {id:11,label:'S11', value:10},{id:12,label:'S12', value:9},
    {id:13,label:'S13', value:8}, {id:14,label:'S14', value:7},
    {id:15,label:'S15', value:6}, {id:16,label:'S16', value:5},
    {id:17,label:'S17', value:4}, {id:18,label:'S18', value:3},
    {id:19,label:'S19', value:2}, {id:20,label:'S20', value:1},
  ],
  edges: [
    // S1 & S2 (top) are nominated by many, nominate few
    {source:3,target:1},{source:4,target:1},{source:5,target:1},{source:7,target:2},
    {source:6,target:2},{source:8,target:2},
    {source:1,target:3},{source:2,target:3},{source:4,target:3},
    {source:1,target:4},{source:5,target:4},{source:9,target:4},
    {source:2,target:5},{source:6,target:5},{source:10,target:5},
    {source:3,target:6},{source:7,target:6},{source:11,target:6},
    {source:4,target:7},{source:8,target:7},{source:12,target:7},
    {source:5,target:8},{source:9,target:8},{source:13,target:8},
    {source:6,target:9},{source:10,target:9},{source:14,target:9},
    {source:7,target:10},{source:11,target:10},{source:15,target:10},
    {source:8,target:11},{source:12,target:11},{source:16,target:11},
    {source:9,target:12},{source:13,target:12},{source:17,target:12},
    {source:10,target:13},{source:14,target:13},{source:18,target:13},
    {source:11,target:14},{source:15,target:14},{source:19,target:14},
    {source:12,target:15},{source:16,target:15},{source:20,target:15},
    {source:13,target:16},{source:17,target:16},
    {source:14,target:17},{source:18,target:17},
    {source:15,target:18},{source:19,target:18},
    {source:16,target:19},{source:20,target:19},
    {source:17,target:20},
    // A few upsets
    {source:1,target:20},{source:20,target:3},{source:15,target:5},
  ],
};

// ── Helper: wire tabs + zoom reset for one figure ────────────────────────────

function wireFigure(suffix, stateObj) {
  const zBtn = document.getElementById('nez-' + suffix);
  if (zBtn) zBtn.addEventListener('click', () => {
    const v = stateObj.viewer;
    if (v) d3.select('#nesv-' + suffix).transition().duration(400)
              .call(v._zoom.transform, d3.zoomIdentity);
  });

  const tabStrip = document.querySelector('.ne-fig-tabs[data-fig="' + suffix + '"]');
  if (!tabStrip) return;
  tabStrip.addEventListener('click', e => {
    const btn = e.target.closest('.ne-tab-btn');
    if (!btn) return;
    tabStrip.querySelectorAll('.ne-tab-btn').forEach(b => b.classList.remove('active'));
    btn.classList.add('active');
    const tab = btn.dataset.tab;
    const graphPane = tabStrip.nextElementSibling; // .ne-fig-graph
    const matrixPane = document.getElementById('nem-' + suffix);
    if (graphPane) graphPane.style.display = tab === 'graph' ? '' : 'none';
    if (matrixPane) {
      matrixPane.style.display = tab === 'matrix' ? '' : 'none';
      if (tab === 'matrix' && stateObj.viewer) stateObj.viewer.renderMatrix(matrixPane);
    }
  });
}

function makeViewer(graph, layout, suffix) {
  const svgSel = '#nesv-' + suffix;
  const infoPanelSel = '#neinf-' + suffix;
  // Reset tab to Graph
  const tabStrip = document.querySelector('.ne-fig-tabs[data-fig="' + suffix + '"]');
  if (tabStrip) {
    tabStrip.querySelectorAll('.ne-tab-btn').forEach((b, i) => b.classList.toggle('active', i === 0));
    const graphPane = tabStrip.nextElementSibling;
    if (graphPane) graphPane.style.display = '';
    const matrixPane = document.getElementById('nem-' + suffix);
    if (matrixPane) matrixPane.style.display = 'none';
  }
  // Reset info panel
  const ip = document.querySelector(infoPanelSel);
  if (ip) ip.innerHTML = '<span class="nip-hint">Hover or click a node for details</span>';
  return new NetworkViewer({ graph, layout, svgSel, infoPanelSel });
}

// ── Orangutan simple ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

const orangState = { viewer: null };
wireFigure('orang', orangState);
orangState.viewer = makeViewer(graphOrangSimple, 'anchored', 'orang');

// ── Orangutan directed ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

const orangDirState = { viewer: null };
wireFigure('orangdir', orangDirState);
orangDirState.viewer = makeViewer(EXAMPLES.orangutan.graph, 'anchored', 'orangdir');

// ── Community examples ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

const commState = { viewer: null };
wireFigure('comm', commState);

// Post-process helpers
function postFootball(g) {
  g.nodes.forEach(n => { n.group = n.value; });
}
function postPolbooks(g) {
  const m = {l: 0, n: 1, c: 2};
  g.nodes.forEach(n => { n.group = m[n.value] ?? 1; });
}
function postRomeo(g) {
  const groupMap = {
    ROMEO:0, MONTAGUE:0, BENVOLIO:0, MERCUTIO:0, BALTHASAR:0, ABRAM:0,
    CAPULET:1, TYBALT:1, JULIET:1, NURSE:1, PETRUCHIO:1, SAMPSON:1, GREGORY:1, PETER:1,
    APOTHECARY:2, PARIS:2, ESCALUS:2,
  };
  g.nodes.forEach(n => { n.group = groupMap[n.label] ?? 2; });
}

const commGraphs = {};  // cache fetched graphs

function loadCommGraph(key) {
  if (commState.viewer) { commState.viewer.destroy(); commState.viewer = null; }

  if (key === 'pollinator') {
    commState.viewer = makeViewer(EXAMPLES.pollinator.graph, 'bipartite', 'comm');
    return;
  }

  const fileMap = { football: 'football.gml', polbooks: 'polbooks.gml', romeo: 'romeo_and_juliet.gml' };
  const layoutMap = { football: 'force', polbooks: 'force', romeo: 'force' };

  if (commGraphs[key]) {
    commState.viewer = makeViewer(commGraphs[key], layoutMap[key], 'comm');
    return;
  }

  fetch('/assets/gml/' + fileMap[key])
    .then(r => r.text())
    .then(text => {
      const g = parseGML(text);
      if (key === 'football') postFootball(g);
      else if (key === 'polbooks') postPolbooks(g);
      else if (key === 'romeo') postRomeo(g);
      commGraphs[key] = g;
      commState.viewer = makeViewer(g, layoutMap[key], 'comm');
    })
    .catch(err => {
      const ip = document.querySelector('#neinf-comm');
      if (ip) ip.innerHTML = '<span style="color:#e74c3c;padding:8px">Failed to load ' + fileMap[key] + ': ' + err.message + '</span>';
    });
}

// Load default
loadCommGraph('football');

document.getElementById('nesel-comm').addEventListener('change', e => {
  loadCommGraph(e.target.value);
});

// ── Hierarchy examples ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

const hierState = { viewer: null };
wireFigure('hier', hierState);

const hierGraphs = {
  bigten:     { graph: graphBigTen,     layout: 'hierarchy' },
  soda:       { graph: graphSoda,       layout: 'hierarchy' },
  chickens:   { graph: EXAMPLES.chickens.graph, layout: 'hierarchy' },
  highschool: { graph: graphHighSchool, layout: 'hierarchy' },
};

function loadHierGraph(key) {
  if (hierState.viewer) { hierState.viewer.destroy(); hierState.viewer = null; }
  const ex = hierGraphs[key];
  if (ex) hierState.viewer = makeViewer(ex.graph, ex.layout, 'hier');
}

loadHierGraph('bigten');

document.getElementById('nesel-hier').addEventListener('change', e => {
  loadHierGraph(e.target.value);
});

  } // end init()

  if (document.readyState === 'loading') {
    document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', init);
  } else {
    init();
  }
})();
</script>
```
